meta.title: An Institutional Perspective: Solana Goes Mainstream

transcript:
Brian Raimondi: So yes, the institutions are here. That's... is that anything else? Are we all set? We're done. Appreciate everybody coming out. Very excited to be chatting with you all today. I am Brian Raimondi, as Julianne said. I head up institutional partnerships at Bitwise. We work very closely with the Solana team. Very excited to be chatting here with my friend Evy today. Evy, please take a moment to introduce yourself.

Evy Theunis: Hi, I'm Evy. I'm here from Singapore. DBS is Southeast Asia's largest bank. We're the third largest private bank in Asia, and we're the second most profitable bank in the world. The 21st largest bank globally. And we've been doing this since 2020. So very excited to talk a bit more about what we've been doing and how it's been going for us.

Brian Raimondi: Absolutely. So I guess we would start with, what's it been like to be working within a large global bank, one of the largest banks across Asia, in digital assets over the past five years? We've had some highs and some lows as an industry, and you all have stuck it through and seen an incredible amount of success. So maybe just walk us through that journey a little bit.

Evy Theunis: Yeah. We started talking about it in 2018. The reason we started talking about it was threefold. The first one was because we thought tokenization was going to kick off much faster than it has. Secondly, we saw demand from our private banking customers who wanted to buy into crypto, but they wanted to do it through a safe platform for the amounts that they were looking to place. And thirdly, we are a very data-driven bank, so we saw outflows to the exchanges and we wanted to bring that back in-house. And that's how our discussion started on building our own exchange. We built our own custody first, which launched in the middle of... in August of 2020, and then the exchange at the end of 2020. And we started very small with Bitcoin, ETH, Bitcoin Cash, and XRP. And we built from there. We launched structured notes and options in 2024. We tokenized those in 2025, and we distribute them through real-world asset licensed players within Singapore now, but we're looking to expand that. That's what we do on the trading side. On the banking side, we've also been supporting the ecosystem since 2020. Singapore came out with the PSA license, and we started providing services to those that were licensed in Singapore, and now we bank most of the big players within the digital asset ecosystem that have presence in Asia. And then on the innovation side, the first project we did was 2016. It was called Project Ubin on payments. And from there, we built out commercially viable use cases. And that's why I said we're the second most profitable bank in the world; we focus on use cases which are commercially viable, demand from our clients, and something that we can really scale. So that... we started with Ubin, and now we've got tokenized deposits, purpose-bound money. When you go to Singapore and you get your payments done via our PayLah app, the rewards are blockchain-based. We just announced with J.P. Morgan that we will look at the framework for interoperability between tokenized deposits, us and them. And we do many more initiatives. We also work on tokenization of financial markets. So as I said, we tokenized our notes on public chain, but we're looking to tokenize many more projects. Again, with a focus on scale and on demand, not so much on supply one by one, really looking at where the demand is. Maybe one more announcement we did recently is that we announced that we will partner with Franklin Templeton and Ripple to allow RLUSD and SG Benji, the Singapore version of Benji, to be traded on the exchange, but also look at how we can do repos with that, how we can use that as collateral for trading, and so on. So those are a few of the things that we've done over the past years.

Brian Raimondi: So Bitwise has a slide that shows institutional adoption across all the banks globally, and there's little box ticks for each one that as new products come online. We have to create new categories consistently for Evy and DBS. So a testament to all the work you guys do in the space.

Evy Theunis: I didn't even answer your question of the industry, and now I realize I said everything that we do. And then you said, "How was it?" So I started in this role on the 1st of November 2022, which wasn't a great time. Nothing going on that day. Something else happened a week later. So it was very interesting. But what I think was very interesting about it was, while it was obviously not fantastic to happen, that's the least to say, it also gave some sort of a reset. And we could work with a lot more partners to look at how can we reshape the way things are run. And we, during the quiet time in 2023, 2024, we could really work out what does the next level of digital assets look like. And we did that very diligently behind the scenes. And I'm very proud of those that we worked with and how far they've come since that time. So we weren't resting on our laurels. We were just not as publicized back then as it is right now.

Brian Raimondi: Bear markets are for building, after all.

Evy Theunis: Yeah. And we did the same thing.

Brian Raimondi: Yeah. Excellent. So over the past two years, things have been a little bit more front and center in terms of institutional adoption. This term that, being running institutional at Bitwise for five years, you go to panels like this, you hear these talks, and institutional adoption gets thrown around like hopium, for lack of a better term. Wasn't really there. Today, it's tangible. You could feel it sort of everywhere you go. It's in the headlines, whether it's sovereign adoption right here in the UAE, wirehouses adopting products from asset managers like Bitwise, corporate participation, which we're talking about here today as well. Kind of the list goes on. So enter Solana, and it does feel like, with the inception of the ETF—read ticker BSOL, everybody, if you want to pull that one up at some point today; shameless plug—we're really starting to see Solana's moment just kind of come to the forefront. So how is DBS approaching that? How are you thinking about that? Three-part question. And are clients asking about that?

Evy Theunis: So institutions coming in, you're right. In 2022, when people said we have mostly institutional clients, my question was, "Who?" Because I'm part of the financial institutions group, and I was like, the vast majority of the ones that we service are not actively participating because we speak to them. So who is it then? And it was most of the time, were digital asset native institutional investors. Fast forward, when you looked at the ETF inflows in Bitcoin to begin with, we looked at the makeup of the investors, and you could really see that already more than half of those were traditional institutional players. You could at the same time see more questions. So for example, when we started launching our notes, you could see a lot more demand from private banks, from more the traditional side.

Brian Raimondi: Hedge funds.

Evy Theunis: Yes, but not limited to hedge funds. And maybe I'll speak a bit more about that. You could really see the more traditional side come in with interest into what is the asset class, how can we deal with it. And the ETFs were a big point in that. And fast forward to now, 10th of October was not a great day, but what you could see was the difference between those that had ETFs versus those that didn't. The difference was quite... really helped to stabilize it because the outflows from the ETFs weren't as big.

Brian Raimondi: Stable hands.

Evy Theunis: Stable hands. And you can see that now with some of the other coins coming in as well, where you will see the same trends come in. My god, something went into my throat.

Brian Raimondi: No doubt. One thing I would add, just in terms of what we're seeing at Bitwise, and I think there's been a market difference in sort of like the crypto Twitter sphere versus the institutional adoption, the wirehouses continuing to work on their IDD, their ODD. We've seen like four approvals from large US/global banks in the past three weeks that do not care if the price of Bitcoin goes down 15%. Like this is something... like they've made a decision, they're here, they're not going anywhere. And it's sort of like they're gung-ho in a way. Charles Schwab is investing. Franklin Templeton obviously leaning in via your partnership. It's just, it's nonstop right now. It's kind of all we can do to keep up with it. And it's not impacted by a small hiccup in the market, I would agree.

Evy Theunis: Yeah. You could really see the difference. Also the volumes on CME, open interest on CME overtook at one point in 2024. So you could really see the difference coming in. And so you see it now as well. And on your question on client demand, yes, so on the exchange, we service our private bank, institutional investors, as well as B2B2C. With the B2B2C, what you could see was there was a lot more demand for Solana, for example. It was actually the first one that came up as one of the coins that they wanted to see more of and that people wanted to be more active in. And the way we think about it, I'll say later because I know there is a question on that.

Brian Raimondi: We didn't plan anything. Actually, I had a set of questions that I wanted to ask Evy, and we were on the way over here in a car and she just completely ripped them all up. So just going off the cuff here. One thing I think it's interesting to talk about, family offices have really led the way from an institutional standpoint, I think, whether it's here in Abu Dhabi, whether it's in Singapore, whether it's in the US. They've been great at sort of accelerating the education and the, ultimately, the adoption. If I'm a family office—I'm not yet, I hope to be at some point—who already owns Bitcoin and dare I say Ethereum, why am I going to think about adding Solana? How does that differentiate my portfolio versus just upsizing my existing allocations and having to learn about this new chain that's just come onto sort of financialization rails?

Evy Theunis: I... we can talk about family offices and we can talk about then the longer tail of the private bank, right? So I'll first talk about how they've been investing. So what we could really see in '23, '24 was people were accumulating. So people were accumulating. Trading was far less. They were holding and seeing what was happening. This year, you can see a lot more trading happening. And the way we discuss it, indeed, many start with Bitcoin, and then there is a lot more interest in what are the alternative altcoins that they could look at. And then you look at market cap, you look at activity, look at the whole ecosystem. And we do a lot of education on that. There are webinars on a quarterly basis, but also when there is any questions, people can just come in. So how would you allocate? As we spoke about, the way we would describe it is Bitcoin is Bitcoin, it's its own category. But then you've got Ethereum, Solana, and you can basically think about it as Apple versus Microsoft or just two different technologies, but both being able to serve a wide need. And I mean, in crypto, we like... we have to think a bit more long term. So the way the team is building it and trying to build out the Solana ecosystem is encouraging.

Brian Raimondi: That's great. How do we feel about... I guess we have a minute and a half left here, so we can go straight to the lightning round. If Solana were a traditional asset, what would it be?

Evy Theunis: Like I said, Microsoft or Apple, right? You choose the one that you want to be.

Brian Raimondi: Yeah. No, no doubt. Maybe quickly, what's the sentiment specifically from Asian clients right now? I feel like they're always a little bit ahead of the curve.

Evy Theunis: Um, I think they're still very active. Still very much looking at it. And what you see interestingly is that clients... it's a lot of clients that traditionally didn't make their money in digital assets. So in the past, you might have had people who were very familiar in crypto and they keep doing it or they have a self-interest. But now you see it as part of the allocation of the portfolio. Just another alternative asset.

Brian Raimondi: Yeah. As it should be. What do you tell a skeptical CIO, whether it's crypto, Solana, digital assets in general, if you have 20 seconds?

Evy Theunis: Well, we were joking that I say you have to do your own research, of course. But many people ask when is it a good time to come in, and I think anytime is good when you think long term. So that's what I usually say.

Brian Raimondi: Dollar cost average is always our go-to. When in doubt, zoom out.

Evy Theunis: Yeah. Dollar cost average.

Brian Raimondi: Awesome. Thanks, Evy. Thanks, everybody.

# DBS, JPMorgan to develop interoperability framework for tokenized deposits

By [Danny Park](https://www.theblock.co/author/danny-park)

[Companies](https://www.theblock.co/category/companies)
• November 11, 2025, 5:13AM EST

Share

Partner offers

The Block may may earn a commission if you use our partner offers, at no extra cost to you.

Start trading cryptoGet a crypto rewards cardGet a crypto loan

#### Quick Take

- DBS and Kinexys by J.P. Morgan are in the early stages of developing an interoperability framework for tokenized deposit transfers across various types of blockchain networks.
- The framework could potentially enable round-the-clock transfers of tokenized deposits across public and permissioned blockchain networks.

Singapore's DBS and Kinexys by J.P. Morgan are developing an interoperability framework to facilitate tokenized deposit transfers between their onchain ecosystems.

This collaboration seeks to create an interoperability layer between the two to allow transactions across both public and permissioned networks, which expands access for clients. Both institutions currently offer clients with round-the-clock liquidity and real-time payment settlement in their respective blockchain-based systems.

According to the press release, clients using JPM Deposit Tokens — built on Ethereum Layer 2 Base — could potentially transact with those on DBS Token Services, which runs on a permissioned blockchain. Such interactions have traditionally been difficult due to limited native interoperability and associated security risks.

"Working with DBS on this initiative is a clear example of how financial institutions can collaborate to further the benefits of tokenized deposits for institutional clients while protecting the singleness of money and ensuring interoperability across markets," said Naveen Mallela, global co-head of Kinexys.

Tokenization has become a major venture among global financial institutions in recent years.

Last month, [BNY Mellon](https://www.theblock.co/post/373655/bny-mellon-weighs-tokenized-deposits-to-address-legacy-payment-constraints), the world's largest custodian by assets under custody, is reportedly exploring tokenized deposit services that would allow clients to make payments over blockchain rails.

Several major UK lenders, including Barclays, Lloyds and HSBC, also [announced](https://www.theblock.co/post/372527/uk-banks-to-pilot-tokenized-sterling-deposits-as-lenders-explore-programmable-payment

# DBS and J.P. Morgan Developing Framework for Instant, Cross-Border Tokenised Payments
By linking their blockchain platforms, DBS and J.P. Morgan plan to expand real-time liquidity and interoperability for institutional clients.
![Fintech News Singapore](https://fintechnews.sg/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/FT-Singapore-32x32.png)[Fintech News Singapore](https://fintechnews.sg/author/fintech-singapore/)November 11, 2025**3 Mins Read

tags.impact_reason: A major bank (DBS) discussing their adoption and use of Solana for tokenization and payments signals growing institutional interest and validation of the Solana ecosystem.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a bank vault as the semantic anchor because it's a universal symbol for security, wealth, and institutional power, directly reflecting DBS Bank's role in the story. The narrative is about this established institution embracing Solana, so I depicted the vault opening to reveal Solana's signature vibrant light. For the homepage text, I integrated 'SOL' into the vault's modern, digital locking mechanism to diegetically represent Solana as the new, high-demand asset being secured by the bank.
visual_idea.description: A massive, circular bank vault door made of intricately machined, brushed stainless steel stands slightly ajar in a grand, dimly lit hall with polished obsidian floors. From the crack in the vault, a brilliant, futuristic green and purple light—the Solana gradient—spills out, casting long, dramatic shadows. Integrated into the door is a sleek, modern digital locking mechanism where a holographic display glows with the letters 'SOL'. The background is a deep, cavernous space with volumetric light shafts cutting through a light haze, emphasizing the monumental scale of the institution.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A massive, circular bank vault door, intricately machined from brushed stainless steel, stands slightly ajar in a grand, dimly lit hall. The floor beneath is polished obsidian, reflecting the sparse light. From the narrow crack in the vault, a brilliant, futuristic light in a Solana gradient of green and purple spills dramatically, casting long, stark shadows across the polished obsidian floor. Integrated into the vault door is a sleek, modern digital locking mechanism, where a holographic display glows vibrantly with the letters 'SOL'. The background recedes into a deep, cavernous space, filled with cinematic volumetric light shafts cutting through a subtle haze, emphasizing the monumental scale of this sanctum-like institution. This hyperrealistic, ultra-detailed image features dramatic chiaroscuro lighting, high dynamic range, and strong cinematic contrast, rendered with photoreal detail and realistic materials. The vertical composition (3:4 aspect ratio) emphasizes height and epic scale, with ample negative space at the top, making the vault feel immense and awe-inspiring. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, ultra-photorealistic.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a bank vault as the semantic anchor because it's a universal symbol for security, wealth, and institutional power, directly reflecting DBS Bank's role in the story. The narrative is about this established institution embracing Solana, so I depicted the vault opening to reveal Solana's signature vibrant light. For the homepage text, I integrated 'SOL' into the vault's modern, digital locking mechanism to diegetically represent Solana as the new, high-demand asset being secured by the bank.
visual_idea.description: A hyper-detailed macro shot focusing on the complex locking mechanism and gears of the steel bank vault door. The metallic texture of the brushed steel is highly visible, with precise machining marks. From the very edge of the frame, the vibrant green and purple light of Solana seeps through the door's seam, creating a powerful color contrast and casting sharp, specular highlights across the cold, intricate metalwork.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A hyper-detailed macro shot focusing on the complex locking mechanism and gears of the steel bank vault door. The metallic texture of the brushed steel is highly visible, with precise machining marks, intricately rendered with the sheen of brushed stainless steel. Adjacent components or reflective surfaces incorporate the deep, mirror-like quality of polished obsidian, subtly reflecting ambient light. From the very edge of the frame, cinematic volumetric lighting in vibrant green and purple hues, characteristic of Solana, dramatically seeps through the door's seam, creating a powerful color contrast and casting sharp, specular highlights across the cold, intricate metalwork and the reflective obsidian. The scene is composed for a perfect 1:1 square aspect ratio, emphasizing symmetry and a central focus, with tight macro framing that fills the entire frame with rich metallic and mechanical textures. The atmosphere evokes the secure, mysterious aura of a sanctum. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic volumetric lighting, dramatic chiaroscuro.

editorial.headline: DBS Bank Reports Institutional Surge in Solana Demand
editorial.standfirst: Southeast Asia’s largest bank reveals Solana has emerged as the primary alternative asset request among its B2B2C clientele. The shift marks a transition from passive Bitcoin accumulation to active diversification in Asian institutional portfolios.
key_takeaways: Client Demand: DBS confirmed Solana is the top requested asset beyond Bitcoin and Ethereum for its B2B2C institutional desk.
Infrastructure: DBS and J.P. Morgan’s Kinexys are developing an interoperability framework for cross-chain tokenized deposits.
New Listings: DBS Exchange will list Ripple’s RLUSD and Franklin Templeton’s Benji token, targeting repo and collateral use cases.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The narrative of institutional adoption has evolved from theoretical interest to tangible infrastructure, with **DBS Bank**—the second most profitable bank globally—leading the charge in Asia. During a fireside chat with **Bitwise**, DBS executive Evy Theunis revealed a critical shift in client behavior: 2023 was a year of accumulation, but 2025 has become a year of active trading and diversification. Most notably, Theunis disclosed that **Solana** has surfaced as the primary asset requested by the bank’s B2B2C clients looking to expand beyond the traditional Bitcoin and Ethereum pairing.

This demand signals a maturing mental model among sophisticated investors. Theunis framed the distinction between Ethereum and Solana not as a winner-take-all battle, but as a technology sector allocation similar to "Apple versus Microsoft." For family offices and private banks in Singapore and the broader Asian market, Solana is no longer viewed as a speculative bet, but as a necessary component of a diversified digital asset portfolio. This aligns with **Bitwise’s** observation that wirehouses and global banks are pressing forward with product approvals regardless of short-term price volatility.

Beyond asset allocation, DBS is aggressively upgrading the plumbing of institutional finance. The bank confirmed a new partnership with **J.P. Morgan’s Kinexys** to build an interoperability framework for tokenized deposits, bridging the gap between permissioned banking ledgers and public blockchains. Furthermore, DBS announced plans to integrate **Franklin Templeton’s** Benji token and **Ripple’s** RLUSD stablecoin, specifically to explore complex financial workflows like repurchase agreements (repos) and collateral management. These moves suggest that institutions are preparing to use digital assets for operational liquidity, not just store-of-value.

The "Asia Premium" remains a leading indicator for global trends. According to Theunis, Asian clients—particularly family offices—are treating digital assets as a standard portfolio allocation rather than a novelty. With the **Bitwise Solana ETF (BSOL)** and similar products smoothing the on-ramp, the infrastructure is now catching up to the capital, setting the stage for Solana to become a staple in institutional holdings alongside Bitcoin.
